21.1.pali: Atha kho dīghatapassī nigaṇṭho yena upālissa gahapatissa nivesanaṁ tenupasaṅkami.
21.1.english: Then Dīgha Tapassī went to Upāli’s home.

21.2.pali: Addasā kho dovāriko dīghatapassiṁ nigaṇṭhaṁ dūratova āgacchantaṁ.
21.2.english: The gatekeeper saw him coming off in the distance

21.5.pali: Ajjatagge upāli gahapati samaṇassa gotamassa sāvakattaṁ upagato.
21.5.english: From now on the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.

21.6.pali: Āvaṭaṁ dvāraṁ nigaṇṭhānaṁ nigaṇṭhīnaṁ, anāvaṭaṁ dvāraṁ bhagavato bhikkhūnaṁ bhikkhunīnaṁ upāsakānaṁ upāsikānaṁ.
21.6.english: His gate is closed to Jain monks and nuns, and opened for the Buddha’s monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen.

21.7.pali: Sace te, bhante, piṇḍakena attho, ettheva tiṭṭha, ettheva te āharissantī”ti.
21.7.english: If you require almsfood, wait here, they will bring it to you.”

21.8.pali: “Na me, āvuso, piṇḍakena attho”ti vatvā tato paṭinivattitvā yena nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ etadavoca:
21.8.english: Saying, “No, worthy man, I do not require almsfood,” he turned back and went to the Jain Ñātika and said to him,

21.9.pali: “saccaṁyeva kho, bhante, yaṁ upāli gahapati samaṇassa gotamassa sāvakattaṁ upagato.
21.9.english: “Sir, it’s really true that Upāli has become Gotama’s disciple.

22.1.pali: Atha kho nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto mahatiyā nigaṇṭhaparisāya saddhiṁ yena upālissa gahapatissa nivesanaṁ tenupasaṅkami.
22.1.english: Then the Jain Ñātika went to Upāli’s home together with a large following of Jain ascetics.

22.2.pali: Addasā kho dovāriko nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ dūratova āgacchantaṁ.
22.2.english: The gatekeeper saw him coming off in the distance

22.5.pali: Ajjatagge upāli gahapati samaṇassa gotamassa sāvakattaṁ upagato.
22.5.english: From now on the householder Upāli has become a disciple of the ascetic Gotama.

22.6.pali: Āvaṭaṁ dvāraṁ nigaṇṭhānaṁ nigaṇṭhīnaṁ, anāvaṭaṁ dvāraṁ bhagavato bhikkhūnaṁ bhikkhunīnaṁ upāsakānaṁ upāsikānaṁ.
22.6.english: His gate is closed to Jain monks and nuns, and opened for the Buddha’s monks, nuns, laymen, and laywomen.

22.7.pali: Sace te, bhante, piṇḍakena attho, ettheva tiṭṭha, ettheva te āharissantī”ti.
22.7.english: If you require almsfood, wait here, they will bring it to you.”

22.8.pali: “Tena hi, samma dovārika, yena upāli gahapati tenupasaṅkama; upasaṅkamitvā upāliṁ gahapatiṁ evaṁ vadehi:
22.8.english: “Well then, my good gatekeeper, go to Upāli and say:

22.9.pali: ‘nigaṇṭho, bhante, nāṭaputto mahatiyā nigaṇṭhaparisāya saddhiṁ bahidvārakoṭṭhake ṭhito;
22.9.english: ‘Sir, the Jain Ñātika is waiting outside the gates together with a large following of Jain ascetics.

23.1.pali: Atha kho upāli gahapati yena majjhimā dvārasālā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā yaṁ tattha āsanaṁ aggañca seṭṭhañca uttamañca paṇītañca tattha sāmaṁ nisīditvā dovārikaṁ āmantesi:
23.1.english: Then Upāli went to the hall of the middle gate, where he sat on the highest and finest seat. He addressed the gatekeeper,

23.2.pali: “tena hi, samma dovārika, yena nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto tenupasaṅkama; upasaṅkamitvā nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ evaṁ vadehi:
23.2.english: “Well then, my good gatekeeper, go to the Jain Ñātika and say to him:

23.4.english: you may enter if you wish.’”

23.5.pali: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho dovāriko upālissa gahapatissa paṭissutvā yena nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ etadavoca:
23.5.english: “Yes, sir,” replied the gatekeeper. He went to the Jain Ñātika and relayed what was said.

23.8.pali: Atha kho nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto mahatiyā nigaṇṭhaparisāya saddhiṁ yena majjhimā dvārasālā tenupasaṅkami.
23.8.english: Then the Jain Ñātika went to the hall of the middle gate together with a large following of Jain ascetics.

24.2.pali: yaṁ sudaṁ pubbe yato passati nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ dūratova āgacchantaṁ disvāna tato paccuggantvā yaṁ tattha āsanaṁ aggañca seṭṭhañca uttamañca paṇītañca taṁ uttarāsaṅgena sammajjitvā pariggahetvā nisīdāpeti so—
24.2.english: Previously, when Upāli saw the Jain Ñātika coming, he would go out to greet him and, having wiped off the highest and finest seat with his upper robe, he would embrace him and sit him down.

24.3.pali: dāni yaṁ tattha āsanaṁ aggañca seṭṭhañca uttamañca paṇītañca tattha sāmaṁ nisīditvā nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ etadavoca:
24.3.english: But today, having seated himself on the highest and finest seat, he said to the Jain Ñātika,

25.1.pali: Evaṁ vutte, nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto upāliṁ gahapatiṁ etadavoca:
25.1.english: When he said this, the Jain Ñātika said to him:

25.3.pali: ‘Gacchāmahaṁ, bhante, samaṇassa gotamassa vādaṁ āropessāmī’ti gantvā mahatāsi vādasaṅghāṭena paṭimukko āgato.
25.3.english: You said: ‘I’ll go and refute the ascetic Gotama’s doctrine.’ But you come back caught in the vast net of his doctrine.

25.4.pali: Seyyathāpi, gahapati, puriso aṇḍahārako gantvā ubbhatehi aṇḍehi āgaccheyya, seyyathā vā pana gahapati puriso akkhikahārako gantvā ubbhatehi akkhīhi āgaccheyya;
25.4.english: Suppose a man went to deliver a pair of testicles, but came back castrated. Or they went to deliver eyes, but came back blinded.

25.5.pali: evameva kho tvaṁ, gahapati, ‘gacchāmahaṁ, bhante, samaṇassa gotamassa vādaṁ āropessāmī’ti gantvā mahatāsi vādasaṅghāṭena paṭimukko āgato.
25.5.english: In the same way, you said: ‘I’ll go and refute the ascetic Gotama’s doctrine.’ But you come back caught in the vast net of his doctrine.

25.6.pali: Āvaṭṭosi kho tvaṁ, gahapati, samaṇena gotamena āvaṭṭaniyā māyāyā”ti.
25.6.english: You’ve been converted by the ascetic Gotama’s conversion trick!”

26.3.pali: piyā me, bhante, ñātisālohitā imāya āvaṭṭaniyā āvaṭṭeyyuṁ; piyānampi me assa ñātisālohitānaṁ dīgharattaṁ hitāya sukhāya;
26.3.english: If my loved ones—relatives and kin—were to be converted by this, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness.

26.4.pali: sabbe cepi, bhante, khattiyā imāya āvaṭṭaniyā āvaṭṭeyyuṁ; sabbesānampissa khattiyānaṁ dīgharattaṁ hitāya sukhāya; sabbe cepi, bhante, brāhmaṇā …pe… vessā …pe… suddā imāya āvaṭṭaniyā āvaṭṭeyyuṁ; sabbesānampissa suddānaṁ dīgharattaṁ hitāya sukhāya;
26.4.english: If all the aristocrats, brahmins, peasants, and menials were to be converted by this, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness.

26.5.pali: sadevako cepi, bhante, loko samārako sabrahmako sassamaṇabrāhmaṇī pajā sadevamanussā imāya āvaṭṭaniyā āvaṭṭeyyuṁ; sadevakassapissa lokassa samārakassa sabrahmakassa sassamaṇabrāhmaṇiyā pajāya sadevamanussāya dīgharattaṁ hitāya sukhāyāti.
26.5.english: If the whole world—with its gods, Māras, and divinities, this population with its ascetics and brahmins, gods and humans—were to be converted by this, it would be for their lasting welfare and happiness.

26.6.pali: Tena hi, bhante, upamaṁ te karissāmi.
26.6.english: Well then, sir, I shall give you a simile.

26.7.pali: Upamāyapidhekacce viññū purisā bhāsitassa atthaṁ ājānanti.
26.7.english: For by means of a simile some sensible people understand the meaning of what is said.

27.1.pali: Bhūtapubbaṁ, bhante, aññatarassa brāhmaṇassa jiṇṇassa vuḍḍhassa mahallakassa daharā māṇavikā pajāpatī ahosi gabbhinī upavijaññā.
27.1.english: Once upon a time there was an old brahmin, elderly and senior. His wife was a young brahmin lady who was pregnant and about to give birth.

27.2.pali: Atha kho, bhante, sā māṇavikā taṁ brāhmaṇaṁ etadavoca,
27.2.english: Then she said to the brahmin,

27.3.pali: ‘gaccha tvaṁ, brāhmaṇa, āpaṇā makkaṭacchāpakaṁ kiṇitvā ānehi, yo me kumārakassa kīḷāpanako bhavissatī’ti.
27.3.english: ‘Go, brahmin, buy a baby monkey from the market and bring it back so it can be a playmate for my child.’

27.4.pali: Evaṁ vutte, so brāhmaṇo taṁ māṇavikaṁ etadavoca:
27.4.english: When she said this, the brahmin said to her,

27.5.english: ‘Wait, my dear, until you give birth.

27.6.pali: Sace tvaṁ, bhoti, kumārakaṁ vijāyissasi, tassā te ahaṁ āpaṇā makkaṭacchāpakaṁ kiṇitvā ānessāmi, yo te kumārakassa kīḷāpanako bhavissati.
27.6.english: If your child is a boy, I’ll buy you a male monkey,

27.7.pali: Sace pana tvaṁ, bhoti, kumārikaṁ vijāyissasi, tassā te ahaṁ āpaṇā makkaṭacchāpikaṁ kiṇitvā ānessāmi, yā te kumārikāya kīḷāpanikā bhavissatī’ti.
27.7.english: but if it’s a girl, I’ll buy a female monkey.’

27.9.pali: tatiyampi kho, bhante, sā māṇavikā taṁ brāhmaṇaṁ etadavoca:
27.9.english: and a third time she said to the brahmin,

28.1.english: “Householder, the king and his retinue know you as

28.2.english: a disciple of the Jain Ñātika.

28.3.english: Whose disciple should we remember you as?”

29.1.pali: Evaṁ vutte, upāli gahapati uṭṭhāyāsanā ekaṁsaṁ uttarāsaṅgaṁ karitvā yena bhagavā tenañjaliṁ paṇāmetvā nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ etadavoca:
29.1.english: When he had spoken, the householder Upāli got up from his seat, arranged his robe over one shoulder, raised his cupped palms in the direction of the Buddha, and said to the Jain Ñātika,

29.2.english: “Well then, sir, hear whose disciple I am:

29.3.english: The attentive one, free of delusion,

29.4.english: with hard-heartedness dissolved, victor in battle;

29.6.english: with the virtue of an elder and the wisdom of a saint,

29.7.english: immaculate in the midst of it all:

29.8.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.

29.9.english: He has no indecision, he’s content,

3.10.pali: “Aññadeva, āvuso gotama, kāyadaṇḍaṁ, aññaṁ vacīdaṇḍaṁ, aññaṁ manodaṇḍan”ti.

3.11.pali: “Imesaṁ pana, tapassi, tiṇṇaṁ daṇḍānaṁ evaṁ paṭivibhattānaṁ evaṁ paṭivisiṭṭhānaṁ katamaṁ daṇḍaṁ nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto mahāsāvajjataraṁ paññapeti pāpassa kammassa kiriyāya pāpassa kammassa pavattiyā, yadi vā kāyadaṇḍaṁ, yadi vā vacīdaṇḍaṁ, yadi vā manodaṇḍan”ti?
3.11.english: “Of the three rods thus analyzed and differentiated, which rod does the Jain Ñātika describe as being the most blameworthy for performing bad deeds: the physical rod, the verbal rod, or the mental rod?”

3.12.pali: “Imesaṁ kho, āvuso gotama, tiṇṇaṁ daṇḍānaṁ evaṁ paṭivibhattānaṁ evaṁ paṭivisiṭṭhānaṁ kāyadaṇḍaṁ nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto mahāsāvajjataraṁ paññapeti pāpassa kammassa kiriyāya pāpassa kammassa pavattiyā, no tathā vacīdaṇḍaṁ, no tathā manodaṇḍan”ti.
3.12.english: “The Jain Ñātika describes the physical rod as being the most blameworthy for performing bad deeds, not so much the verbal rod or the mental rod.”

3.13.english: “Do you say the physical rod, Tapassī?”

3.14.english: “I say the physical rod, Reverend Gotama.”

3.15.english: “Do you say the physical rod, Tapassī?”

3.16.english: “I say the physical rod, Reverend Gotama.”

3.17.english: “Do you say the physical rod, Tapassī?”

3.18.english: “I say the physical rod, Reverend Gotama.”

3.19.pali: Itiha bhagavā dīghatapassiṁ nigaṇṭhaṁ imasmiṁ kathāvatthusmiṁ yāvatatiyakaṁ patiṭṭhāpesi.
3.19.english: Thus the Buddha made Dīgha Tapassī stand by this point up to the third time.

30.1.pali: “Kadā saññūḷhā pana te, gahapati, ime samaṇassa gotamassa vaṇṇā”ti?
30.1.english: “But when did you compose these praises of the ascetic Gotama’s beautiful qualities, householder?”

30.2.pali: “Seyyathāpi, bhante, nānāpupphānaṁ mahāpuppharāsi, tamenaṁ dakkho mālākāro vā mālākārantevāsī vā vicittaṁ mālaṁ gantheyya;
30.2.english: “Sir, suppose there was a large heap of many different flowers. A deft garland-maker or their apprentice could tie them into a colorful garland.

30.3.pali: evameva kho, bhante, so bhagavā anekavaṇṇo anekasatavaṇṇo.
30.3.english: In the same way, the Buddha has many beautiful qualities to praise, many hundreds of such qualities.

30.4.pali: Ko hi, bhante, vaṇṇārahassa vaṇṇaṁ na karissatī”ti?
30.4.english: Who, sir, would not praise the praiseworthy?”

31.1.pali: Atha kho nigaṇṭhassa nāṭaputtassa bhagavato sakkāraṁ asahamānassa tattheva uṇhaṁ lohitaṁ mukhato uggacchīti.
31.1.english: Unable to bear this honor paid to the Buddha, the Jain Ñātika spewed hot blood from his mouth there and then.

4.10.pali: “Imesaṁ panāvuso gotama, tiṇṇaṁ kammānaṁ evaṁ paṭivibhattānaṁ evaṁ paṭivisiṭṭhānaṁ katamaṁ kammaṁ mahāsāvajjataraṁ paññapesi pāpassa kammassa kiriyāya pāpassa kammassa pavattiyā, yadi vā kāyakammaṁ, yadi vā vacīkammaṁ, yadi vā manokamman”ti?
4.10.english: “Of the three deeds thus analyzed and differentiated, which deed do you describe as being the most blameworthy for performing bad deeds: physical deeds, verbal deeds, or mental deeds?”

4.11.pali: “Imesaṁ kho ahaṁ, tapassi, tiṇṇaṁ kammānaṁ evaṁ paṭivibhattānaṁ evaṁ paṭivisiṭṭhānaṁ manokammaṁ mahāsāvajjataraṁ paññapemi pāpassa kammassa kiriyāya pāpassa kammassa pavattiyā, no tathā kāyakammaṁ, no tathā vacīkamman”ti.
4.11.english: “I describe mental deeds as being the most blameworthy for performing bad deeds, not so much physical deeds or verbal deeds.”

4.12.english: “Do you say mental deeds, Reverend Gotama?”

4.14.english: “Do you say mental deeds, Reverend Gotama?”

4.16.english: “Do you say mental deeds, Reverend Gotama?”

4.18.pali: Itiha dīghatapassī nigaṇṭho bhagavantaṁ imasmiṁ kathāvatthusmiṁ yāvatatiyakaṁ patiṭṭhāpetvā uṭṭhāyāsanā yena nigaṇṭho nāṭaputto tenupasaṅkami.
4.18.english: Thus the Jain ascetic Dīgha Tapassī made the Buddha stand by this point up to the third time, after which he got up from his seat and went to see the Jain Ñātika.

7.10.pali: Seyyathāpi nāma kuñjaro saṭṭhihāyano gambhīraṁ pokkharaṇiṁ ogāhetvā sāṇadhovikaṁ nāma kīḷitajātaṁ kīḷati; evamevāhaṁ samaṇaṁ gotamaṁ sāṇadhovikaṁ maññe kīḷitajātaṁ kīḷissāmi.
7.10.english: I’ll play a game of hemp-washing with the ascetic Gotama, like a sixty-year-old elephant would plunge into a deep lotus pond and play a game of hemp-washing!

7.11.pali: Handa cāhaṁ, bhante, gacchāmi samaṇassa gotamassa imasmiṁ kathāvatthusmiṁ vādaṁ āropessāmī”ti.
7.11.english: Sir, I’d better go and refute the ascetic Gotama’s doctrine on this point.”

7.12.pali: “Gaccha tvaṁ, gahapati, samaṇassa gotamassa imasmiṁ kathāvatthusmiṁ vādaṁ āropehi.
7.12.english: “Go, householder, refute the ascetic Gotama’s doctrine on this point.

7.13.pali: Ahaṁ vā hi, gahapati, samaṇassa gotamassa vādaṁ āropeyyaṁ, dīghatapassī vā nigaṇṭho, tvaṁ vā”ti.
7.13.english: For either I should do so, or Dīgha Tapassī, or you.”

9.10.english: “Sir, did the Jain ascetic Dīgha Tapassī come here?”

9.12.pali: “Ahu kho pana te, bhante, dīghatapassinā nigaṇṭhena saddhiṁ kocideva kathāsallāpo”ti?
9.12.english: “But did you have some discussion with him?”

9.13.pali: “Ahu kho me, gahapati, dīghatapassinā nigaṇṭhena saddhiṁ kocideva kathāsallāpo”ti.

9.14.pali: “Yathā kathaṁ pana te, bhante, ahu dīghatapassinā nigaṇṭhena saddhiṁ kocideva kathāsallāpo”ti?
9.14.english: “And what kind of discussion did you have with him?”

9.15.pali: Atha kho bhagavā yāvatako ahosi dīghatapassinā nigaṇṭhena saddhiṁ kathāsallāpo taṁ sabbaṁ upālissa gahapatissa ārocesi.
9.15.english: Then the Buddha informed Upāli of all they had discussed.

11.10.pali: Bhāsitā kho pana te, gahapati, esā vācā:
11.10.english: But you said that you would debate on the basis of truth.”

11.13.pali: “Kiñcāpi, bhante, bhagavā evamāha, atha kho kāyadaṇḍova mahāsāvajjataro pāpassa kammassa kiriyāya pāpassa kammassa pavattiyā, no tathā vacīdaṇḍo, no tathā manodaṇḍo”ti.
11.13.english: “Even though the Buddha says this, still the physical rod is the most blameworthy for performing bad deeds, not so much the verbal rod or the mental rod.”

12.10.pali: “Manasi karohi, gahapati, manasi karitvā kho, gahapati, byākarohi.
12.10.english: “Think about it, householder! You should think before answering.

12.11.pali: Na kho te sandhiyati purimena vā pacchimaṁ, pacchimena vā purimaṁ.
12.11.english: What you said before and what you said after don’t match up.

12.12.pali: Bhāsitā kho pana te, gahapati, esā vācā:
12.12.english: But you said that you would debate on the basis of truth.”

12.15.pali: “Kiñcāpi, bhante, bhagavā evamāha, atha kho kāyadaṇḍova mahāsāvajjataro pāpassa kammassa kiriyāya pāpassa kammassa pavattiyā, no tathā vacīdaṇḍo, no tathā manodaṇḍo”ti.
12.15.english: “Even though the Buddha says this, still the physical rod is the most blameworthy for performing bad deeds, not so much the verbal rod or the mental rod.”

13.10.pali: “Dasapi, bhante, purisā, vīsampi, bhante, purisā, tiṁsampi, bhante, purisā, cattārīsampi, bhante, purisā, paññāsampi, bhante, purisā nappahonti yāvatikā imissā nāḷandāya pāṇā te ekena khaṇena ekena muhuttena ekaṁ maṁsakhalaṁ ekaṁ maṁsapuñjaṁ kātuṁ.
13.10.english: “Sir, even ten, twenty, thirty, forty, or fifty men couldn’t do that.

13.11.english: How significant is one measly man?”

13.13.pali: idha āgaccheyya samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā iddhimā cetovasippatto.
13.13.english: Suppose an ascetic or brahmin with psychic power, who has achieved mastery of the mind, were to come along

13.15.pali: ‘ahaṁ imaṁ nāḷandaṁ ekena manopadosena bhasmaṁ karissāmī’ti.
13.15.english: ‘I will reduce Nāḷandā to ashes with a single malevolent act of will!’

13.17.pali: pahoti nu kho so samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā iddhimā cetovasippatto imaṁ nāḷandaṁ ekena manopadosena bhasmaṁ kātun”ti?

13.18.pali: “Dasapi, bhante, nāḷandā, vīsampi nāḷandā, tiṁsampi nāḷandā, cattārīsampi nāḷandā, paññāsampi nāḷandā pahoti so samaṇo vā brāhmaṇo vā iddhimā cetovasippatto ekena manopadosena bhasmaṁ kātuṁ.
13.18.english: “Sir, an ascetic or brahmin with psychic power, who has achieved mastery of the mind, could reduce ten, twenty, thirty, forty, or fifty Nāḷandās to ashes with a single malevolent act of will.

13.19.english: How significant is one measly Nāḷandā?”

13.20.pali: “Manasi karohi, gahapati, manasi karitvā kho, gahapati, byākarohi.
13.20.english: “Think about it, householder! You should think before answering.

13.21.pali: Na kho te sandhiyati purimena vā pacchimaṁ, pacchimena vā purimaṁ.
13.21.english: What you said before and what you said after don’t match up.

13.22.pali: Bhāsitā kho pana te, gahapati, esā vācā:
13.22.english: But you said that you would debate on the basis of truth.”

13.25.pali: “Kiñcāpi, bhante, bhagavā evamāha, atha kho kāyadaṇḍova mahāsāvajjataro pāpassa kammassa kiriyāya pāpassa kammassa pavattiyā, no tathā vacīdaṇḍo, no tathā manodaṇḍo”ti.
13.25.english: “Even though the Buddha says this, still the physical rod is the most blameworthy for performing bad deeds, not so much the verbal rod or the mental rod.”

13.27.pali: sutaṁ te daṇḍakīraññaṁ kāliṅgāraññaṁ majjhāraññaṁ mātaṅgāraññaṁ araññaṁ araññabhūtan”ti?
13.27.english: Have you heard how the wildernesses of Daṇḍaka, Kaliṅga, Mejjha, and Mātaṅga came to be that way?”

13.28.pali: “Evaṁ, bhante, sutaṁ me daṇḍakīraññaṁ kāliṅgāraññaṁ majjhāraññaṁ mātaṅgāraññaṁ araññaṁ araññabhūtan”ti.

17.10.pali: Esāhaṁ, bhante, tatiyampi bhagavantaṁ saraṇaṁ gacchāmi dhammañca bhikkhusaṅghañca.
17.10.english: For a third time, I go for refuge to the Buddha, to the teaching, and to the mendicant Saṅgha.

17.11.pali: Upāsakaṁ maṁ bhagavā dhāretu ajjatagge pāṇupetaṁ saraṇaṁ gatan”ti.
17.11.english: From this day forth, may the Buddha remember me as a lay follower who has gone for refuge for life.”

20.10.english: “It is impossible, Tapassī, it cannot happen that Upāli could become Gotama’s disciple.

20.11.english: But it is possible that Gotama could become Upāli’s disciple.”

20.12.pali: “Handāhaṁ, bhante, gacchāmi yāva jānāmi yadi vā upāli gahapati samaṇassa gotamassa sāvakattaṁ upagato yadi vā no”ti.
20.12.english: “Well, sir, I’d better go and find out whether or not Upāli has become Gotama’s disciple.”

20.13.pali: “Gaccha tvaṁ, tapassi, jānāhi yadi vā upāli gahapati samaṇassa gotamassa sāvakattaṁ upagato yadi vā no”ti.
20.13.english: “Go, Tapassī, and find out whether or not Upāli has become Gotama’s disciple.”

21.10.pali: Etaṁ kho te ahaṁ, bhante, nālatthaṁ, na kho me, bhante, ruccati yaṁ upāli gahapati samaṇassa gotamassa vādaṁ āropeyya.
21.10.english: Sir, I couldn’t get you to accept that it wasn’t a good idea for the householder Upāli to rebut the ascetic Gotama’s doctrine.

21.11.pali: Samaṇo hi, bhante, gotamo māyāvī āvaṭṭaniṁ māyaṁ jānāti yāya aññatitthiyānaṁ sāvake āvaṭṭetīti.
21.11.english: For the ascetic Gotama is deceitful. He knows a conversion trick, and uses it to convert the disciples of other religions.

21.12.pali: Āvaṭṭo kho te, bhante, upāli gahapati samaṇena gotamena āvaṭṭaniyā māyāyā”ti.
21.12.english: The householder Upāli has been converted by the ascetic Gotama’s conversion trick!”

21.13.pali: “Aṭṭhānaṁ kho etaṁ, tapassi, anavakāso yaṁ upāli gahapati samaṇassa gotamassa sāvakattaṁ upagaccheyya.
21.13.english: “It is impossible, Tapassī, it cannot happen that Upāli could become Gotama’s disciple.

21.14.pali: Ṭhānañca kho etaṁ vijjati yaṁ samaṇo gotamo upālissa gahapatissa sāvakattaṁ upagaccheyyā”ti.
21.14.english: But it is possible that Gotama could become Upāli’s disciple.”

21.15.pali: Dutiyampi kho dīghatapassī nigaṇṭho nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ etadavoca:

21.18.pali: Tatiyampi kho dīghatapassī nigaṇṭho nigaṇṭhaṁ nāṭaputtaṁ etadavoca:
21.18.english: and a third time, Dīgha Tapassī told the Jain Ñātika

21.22.pali: “Handa cāhaṁ, tapassi, gacchāmi yāva cāhaṁ sāmaṁyeva jānāmi yadi vā upāli gahapati samaṇassa gotamassa sāvakattaṁ upagato yadi vā no”ti.
21.22.english: Well, Tapassī, I’d better go and find out for myself whether or not Upāli has become Gotama’s disciple.”

22.11.pali: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho dovāriko nigaṇṭhassa nāṭaputtassa paṭissutvā yena upāli gahapati tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā upāliṁ gahapatiṁ etadavoca:
22.11.english: “Yes, sir,” replied the gatekeeper. He went to Upāli and relayed what was said.

22.12.pali: “nigaṇṭho, bhante, nāṭaputto mahatiyā nigaṇṭhaparisāya saddhiṁ bahidvārakoṭṭhake ṭhito;

22.14.pali: “Tena hi, samma dovārika, majjhimāya dvārasālāya āsanāni paññapehī”ti.
22.14.english: Upāli said to him, “Well, then, my good gatekeeper, prepare seats in the hall of the middle gate.”

22.15.pali: “Evaṁ, bhante”ti kho dovāriko upālissa gahapatissa paṭissutvā majjhimāya dvārasālāya āsanāni paññapetvā yena upāli gahapati tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā upāliṁ gahapatiṁ etadavoca:
22.15.english: “Yes, sir,” replied the gatekeeper. He did as he was asked, then returned to Upāli and said,

22.16.english: “Sir, seats have been prepared in the hall of the middle gate.

27.10.pali: ‘gaccha tvaṁ, brāhmaṇa, āpaṇā makkaṭacchāpakaṁ kiṇitvā ānehi, yo me kumārakassa kīḷāpanako bhavissatī’ti.
27.10.english: ‘Go, brahmin, buy a baby monkey from the market and bring it back so it can be a playmate for my child.’

27.11.pali: Atha kho, bhante, so brāhmaṇo tassā māṇavikāya sāratto paṭibaddhacitto āpaṇā makkaṭacchāpakaṁ kiṇitvā ānetvā taṁ māṇavikaṁ etadavoca:
27.11.english: Then that brahmin, because of his love for the brahmin lady, bought a male baby monkey at the market, brought it to her, and said,

27.12.pali: ‘ayaṁ te, bhoti, āpaṇā makkaṭacchāpako kiṇitvā ānīto, yo te kumārakassa kīḷāpanako bhavissatī’ti.
27.12.english: ‘I’ve bought this male baby monkey for you so it can be a playmate for your child.’

27.13.pali: Evaṁ vutte, bhante, sā māṇavikā taṁ brāhmaṇaṁ etadavoca:
27.13.english: When he said this, she said to him,

27.14.pali: ‘gaccha tvaṁ, brāhmaṇa, imaṁ makkaṭacchāpakaṁ ādāya yena rattapāṇi rajakaputto tenupasaṅkama; upasaṅkamitvā rattapāṇiṁ rajakaputtaṁ evaṁ vadehi—
27.14.english: ‘Go, brahmin, take this monkey to Rattapāṇi the dyer and say,

27.15.pali: icchāmahaṁ, samma rattapāṇi, imaṁ makkaṭacchāpakaṁ pītāvalepanaṁ nāma raṅgajātaṁ rajitaṁ ākoṭitapaccākoṭitaṁ ubhatobhāgavimaṭṭhan’ti.
27.15.english: “Worthy Rattapāṇi, I wish to have this monkey dyed the color of yellow greasepaint, pounded and re-pounded, and pressed on both sides.”’

27.16.pali: Atha kho, bhante, so brāhmaṇo tassā māṇavikāya sāratto paṭibaddhacitto taṁ makkaṭacchāpakaṁ ādāya yena rattapāṇi rajakaputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā rattapāṇiṁ rajakaputtaṁ etadavoca:
27.16.english: Then that brahmin, because of his love for the brahmin lady, took the monkey to Rattapāṇi the dyer and said,

27.17.pali: ‘icchāmahaṁ, samma rattapāṇi, imaṁ makkaṭacchāpakaṁ pītāvalepanaṁ nāma raṅgajātaṁ rajitaṁ ākoṭitapaccākoṭitaṁ ubhatobhāgavimaṭṭhan’ti.
27.17.english: ‘Worthy Rattapāṇi, I wish to have this monkey dyed the color of yellow greasepaint, pounded and re-pounded, and pressed on both sides.’

27.18.pali: Evaṁ vutte, bhante, rattapāṇi rajakaputto taṁ brāhmaṇaṁ etadavoca:
27.18.english: When he said this, Rattapāṇi said to him,

27.19.pali: ‘ayaṁ kho te, bhante, makkaṭacchāpako raṅgakkhamo hi kho, no ākoṭanakkhamo, no vimajjanakkhamo’ti.
27.19.english: ‘Sir, this monkey can endure a dying, but not a pounding or a pressing.’

27.20.pali: Evameva kho, bhante, bālānaṁ nigaṇṭhānaṁ vādo raṅgakkhamo hi kho bālānaṁ no paṇḍitānaṁ, no anuyogakkhamo, no vimajjanakkhamo.
27.20.english: In the same way, the doctrine of the foolish Jains looks fine initially—for fools, not for the astute—but can’t endure being scrutinized or pressed.

27.21.pali: Atha kho, bhante, so brāhmaṇo aparena samayena navaṁ dussayugaṁ ādāya yena rattapāṇi rajakaputto tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā rattapāṇiṁ rajakaputtaṁ etadavoca:
27.21.english: Then some time later that brahmin took a new pair of garments to Rattapāṇi the dyer and said,

27.22.pali: ‘icchāmahaṁ, samma rattapāṇi, imaṁ navaṁ dussayugaṁ pītāvalepanaṁ nāma raṅgajātaṁ rajitaṁ ākoṭitapaccākoṭitaṁ ubhatobhāgavimaṭṭhan’ti.
27.22.english: ‘Worthy Rattapāṇi, I wish to have this new pair of garments dyed the color of yellow greasepaint, pounded and re-pounded, and pressed on both sides.’

27.23.pali: Evaṁ vutte, bhante, rattapāṇi rajakaputto taṁ brāhmaṇaṁ etadavoca:
27.23.english: When he said this, Rattapāṇi said to him,

27.24.pali: ‘idaṁ kho te, bhante, navaṁ dussayugaṁ raṅgakkhamañceva ākoṭanakkhamañca vimajjanakkhamañcā’ti.
27.24.english: ‘Sir, this pair of garments can endure a dying, a pounding, and a pressing.’

27.25.pali: Evameva kho, bhante, tassa bhagavato vādo arahato sammāsambuddhassa raṅgakkhamo ceva paṇḍitānaṁ no bālānaṁ, anuyogakkhamo ca vimajjanakkhamo cā”ti.
27.25.english: In the same way, the doctrine of the Buddha looks fine initially—for the astute, not for fools—and it can endure being scrutinized and pressed.”

29.10.english: joyful, he has spat out the world’s bait;

29.11.english: he has completed the ascetic’s task as a man,

29.12.english: a man who bears his final body;

29.14.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.

29.15.english: He’s free of doubt, he’s skillful,

29.16.english: he’s a trainer, an excellent charioteer;

29.19.english: he has cut off conceit, he’s a hero:

29.20.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.

29.23.english: he is the maker of sanctuary, knowledgeable,

29.25.english: he has slipped his chains and is liberated:

29.26.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.

29.28.english: he’s ended the fetters and is liberated;

29.29.english: he’s skilled in debate and cleansed,

29.32.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.

29.33.english: He is the seventh seer, free of deceit,

29.34.english: with three knowledges, he has attained to divinity,

29.35.english: he has bathed, he knows philology,

29.36.english: he’s tranquil, he understands what is known;

29.37.english: he is Sakka the Able, Purindada the Firstgiver:

29.38.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.

29.40.english: he has attained the goal and explains it;

29.42.english: neither leaning forward nor pulling back,

29.44.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.

29.46.english: no inner thoughts arising, he is pure,

29.48.english: secluded, he has reached the peak,

29.49.english: crossed over, he helps others across:

29.50.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.

29.51.english: He’s peaceful, his wisdom is vast,

29.52.english: with great wisdom, he’s free of greed;

29.53.english: he is the Realized One, the Holy One,

29.56.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.

29.57.english: He has cut off craving and is awakened,

29.59.english: a mighty spirit worthy of offerings,

29.61.english: grand, he has reached the peak of glory:

29.62.english: he is the Buddha, and I am his disciple.”
